---
title: "FEMA Hazus — GIS and risk-modeling software"
subtitle: "Software development in support of a natural-hazard loss-estimation program"
---

::: {.tags .project-tags}
[GIS]{.tag} [Risk modeling]{.tag} [C#]{.tag} [C++]{.tag} [SQL Server]{.tag} [ArcGIS]{.tag}
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
This page is intentionally high-level. It describes the kind of work and the
engineering involved, not specifics of any internal system, data, or
deliverable.
:::

## Problem

FEMA's Hazus program estimates potential losses from natural hazards
(earthquakes, floods, hurricanes, and related perils) by combining hazard data
with inventories of buildings, infrastructure, and population. A lot of parts
have to stay consistent for the output to mean anything: the geospatial data,
the model logic, and the databases underneath. A change in one place can quietly
shift results downstream, so much of the job is keeping the model accurate and
maintainable as the data and methods change.

## Approach

I work on the software and data side: implementing and maintaining GIS-driven
hazard and risk workflows, keeping the underlying databases current and
consistent, and checking that changes produce correct, explainable results, not
just code that runs without erroring.

A representative piece of work: I led a roughly 2,000-event validation study
comparing two hazard-data source formats, using descriptive statistics and
confidence intervals to measure agreement. It surfaced a geospatial
data-processing defect that got fixed before deployment. I also wrote a Python
geospatial abstraction that standardizes coordinate-reference handling, spatial
joins, and feature-class I/O across hazard modules, and I keep the SQL Server
databases behind these workflows in shape with scripted QA checks.

## Methods and tools

- **GIS software development.** Geospatial workflows built around ArcGIS-based
  data and tooling.
- **Hazard and risk modeling support.** Implementing and maintaining the logic
  that turns hazard and inventory data into loss estimates.
- **Data engineering.** Database updates and maintenance in SQL Server, keeping
  spatial and tabular data consistent.
- **Validation.** Checking model and data changes for correctness before they
  ship, which is the part that matters most when people make decisions on the
  output.
- **Stack.** C#, C++, SQL Server, and ArcGIS-related geospatial tooling.

## Status

Ongoing professional work.

## What this demonstrates

- Production GIS software engineering against a real, widely used risk model.
- Working across application code, spatial data, and databases at once.
- Taking validation and correctness seriously in a setting where the results
  actually get used.
